_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 1
questions.prompt: Which line makes the `random` module available under its own name?
questions.explanation: `import random` binds the module name. Its functions are then reached with a dot, such as `random.randint(1, 6)`.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 2
choices: When this file is run directly
Every time the file is imported
questions.explanation: Python sets `__name__` to `"__main__"` for the file that starts the program. Imported modules receive their module names instead.

questions.title: Trace Modules & Imports
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Importing the module keeps the source of `sqrt` clear. The dot says that the function comes from math. The resulting output is '5.0'.

questions.title: Modules & Imports practice 5
questions.prompt: Why prefer `import math; math.sqrt(16)` over `from math import *`?
choices: The wildcard dumps every name in and can quietly overwrite one you defined
The wildcard import is a syntax error
sqrt does not exist in the math module
questions.explanation: `from math import *` pulls in every public name at once, which hides where a name came from and can shadow something you already defined (for example, your own `sqrt`). `import math` keeps the boundary visible: `math.sqrt` says exactly where it lives. Both call the same function at the same speed.

questions.title: Modules & Imports concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Imported files can run code"?
choices: Statements at the top level run when Python imports the file. Place demo code and direct user input inside a main guard so importing the module does not start them.
questions.explanation: A Python file is a module. Use `import module` to keep its name visible, or `from module import name` to bring in one item. That reasoning supports the answer: Statements at the top level run when Python imports the file. Place demo code and direct user input inside a main guard so importing the module does not start them.

questions.title: Modules & Imports concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Modules & Imports practice?
choices: Every time the file is imported
Group code by one clear purpose.
questions.explanation: A Python file is a module. Use `import module` to keep its name visible, or `from module import name` to bring in one item. That reasoning supports the answer: Group code by one clear purpose.

questions.title: Modules & Imports concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Modules & Imports code?
choices: Every time the file is imported
Prefer imports that show where a name came from.
questions.explanation: A Python file is a module. Use `import module` to keep its name visible, or `from module import name` to bring in one item. That reasoning supports the answer: Prefer imports that show where a name came from.

questions.title: Modules & Imports concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Modules & Imports mistake?
questions.explanation: A Python file is a module. Use `import module` to keep its name visible, or `from module import name` to bring in one item. That reasoning supports the answer: Avoid wildcard imports.

questions.title: Modules & Imports concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Modules & Imports. Which advice is correct?
questions.explanation: A Python file is a module. Use `import module` to keep its name visible, or `from module import name` to bring in one item. That reasoning supports the answer: Keep top-level work small.

questions.title: Modules & Imports concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about Modules & Imports?
choices: Put demos and command-line input behind a main guard.
questions.explanation: A Python file is a module. Use `import module` to keep its name visible, or `from module import name` to bring in one item. That reasoning supports the answer: Put demos and command-line input behind a main guard.
